# Utilizing TransformEEG Technology with SSL to improve model generalizability in Parkinson’s Disease Detection 

# *Instructions*

Proposals must be specific and detailed so the direction of the research is clear. If you’re not sure about a given item, please mark it as such and we can discuss.

Add text using your assigned color for all tabs in this doc:  
mentor, Johnathan, Inesh Bose, Jithin Suresh, Karolina Torbus

# Relevant Past Papers

* Things to include for each paper:  
  * One sentence summary of the paper  
  * Gap / limitation of the paper

TransformEEG (Del Pup et al., 2025\)

* **Summary**: This paper introduces a Convolutional Transformer model (TransformEEG) that is meant to enhance generalizability in EEG Parkinson’s disease detection by using channel specific tokenization  
* **Gap**: A limitation of the paper is that it relies on supervised learning and labeled datasets which are scarce in clinical settings limiting its robustness and scalability for real world settings.

SelfEEG (Del Pup et al., 2023a)

* **Summary**: This paper proposes SelfEEG, a python library that is meant to facilitate self-supervised learning for EEG data as a versatile deep learning tool.

* Relevant documentation:  
  * LLL

Self-supervised Learning for Electroencephalogram (Weng et al., 2024\)

* **Summary:** This paper gives a very comprehensive survey of SSL for EEG analysis and even gives options for practical tasks and future works that can be done.  
* **Gap:** The paper mentions pathology detection as a future work for SSL but does not pursue it leaving the topic open for us to move forward.

# Motivation

* Things to include:  
  * What limitation or problem are you solving and how do you know it exists  
  * Why is this limitation important  
  * Why does your idea solve it  
  * Why would your idea probably work

Limitations of supervised learning is that the datasets used in order to train TransformEEG are labeled but lack in size, and therefore results in low generalizability. The model relies on classified datasets and therefore overfitting if a possibility in a situation where patient results vary increasingly. In order to increase the scalability of the model, more datasets would need to be classified by medical experts which is not only time-consuming but also expensive. This limitation is made clear in the original paper which solely uses small but labeled datasets, whereas OpenNuero provides much more datasets which are unlabeled and of potential use as well. The reason this limitation is important and should be focused on is due to how useful the model may be in actuality when trained using supervised learning only. Supervised learning may result in overfitting on certain patient data. Parkinson’s disease is an illness which could result in several different variations of EEG results, thus overfitting could result in inaccuracy in situations where patients' symptoms differ enough to not be caught by the model. Though this type of experiment has been done before, In order to solve this problem, we  propose self-supervised learning on the prior datasets used by the original paper. This in turn would increase the model’s generalizability and prevent overfitting on labeled datasets. Locating EEG data which is unlabeled would also be less onerous than finding or creating labeled datasets. Since self-supervised learning relies on datasets which are not labeled on whether the patient is healthy or affected by Parkinson’s, training the TransformEEG model using this type of learning would be beneficial due to the lack of large labeled datasets. Not only would this make the experiment and training more cost-effective but the datasets which are suitable for use would increase in size therefore diversifying the variations of EEG data the model would be able to detect. Therefore, by using self-supervised learning, we effectively reduce overfitting and increase model robustness. . 

# Key Ideas/Contributions/Novelty

* What are we doing that hasn’t been done before? Things to consider:  
  * What is the novelty of the idea with respect to the existing literature? What is “new”?  
  * What is the main contribution (to science) of this paper? Are we creating an improved method or a benchmark? What new insights or findings are we providing? What research question are we answering that was not answered? 

While existing literature has gone into the idea of supervised learning with Parkinson’s disease, our novelty comes from the idea of using self-supervised learning with the TransformEEG architecture to detect Parkinson’s disease. While the original TransformEEG work uses supervised learning with labeled datasets, we are able to utilize unlabeled EEG data for pretraining to improve the model’s generalizability and robustness for clinical settings with limited data. Our paper contributes to improving the generalizability of transformer EEG models in disease detection by showing the benefits of self supervised pretraining.   
	

# Methods

* How does your idea work? Describe the way you will get your results from the initial step. Make a diagram. 

Collection of Data:

* In order to conduct self-supervised learning, we find unlabeled EEG datasets linked to Parkinson’s detection from OpenNuero’s database  
  * Filter out noise and artifacts from data  
* Incorporate one dataset used by the original TransformEEG paper  
  * Already pretrained onto TransformEEG

Training the TransformEEG model using SSL:

* Use of **SelfEEG** Python library during the training stage of the model is beneficial as it reduces time-consuming processes  
  * Contrastive Learning Algorithm used since SelfEEG provides pipelines suited to Contrastive algorithms

Related Works: SelfEEG: A Python library for Self-Supervised Learning in  
Electroencephalography\[1\], TransformEEG: Towards Improving Model Generalizability in Deep  
Learning-based EEG Parkinson’s Disease Detection\[2\], 

TransformEEG Architecture:  
![][image1]

# Experimental Setup

* How are you going to test your idea to prove that it works?  
  * Think about social science experiments where researchers have a plan of what to make participants do, what to ask them and how to calculate results based on the responses.  
  * What are your baselines for comparison? i.e control group  
  * What models? What datasets? What metrics (if not accuracy)?

We are comparing the results of the TransformEEG paper to try and increase the accuracy and generalizability of the TransformEEG architecture. We specifically want to use our self-supervised learning architecture to improve upon the results found in the TransformEEG paper, such as the highest balanced accuracy median of 78.45%. We are utilizing some of the datasets used in the original paper, as well as our own unlabeled datasets, to see if we can improve upon how accurate the model is on detecting parkinsonism from EEGs. Other baselines we might be able to implement would be compared with other methods of parkinsonism detection from different papers, such as from drawings. 

* What additional analysis do you plan on doing?  
  * How are you going to prove that any improvements in the system are because of your idea alone, i.e. reduce confounding factors and do ablation testing  
  * How does your idea impact the system? What metrics are you quantifying with?   
    * Visualizations? Statistics?

We plan to use statistics to quantify our improvements and we will compare with the original TransformEEG paper to see if we create better results. Since the only change between their paper and ours will be the self-supervised learning architecture, it will prove that our use of SSL will increase generalizability and accuracy.

# 3\. Datasets and Evaluation

* Which datasets are you going to use to evaluate your method? Or, are you creating your own?  
  * Reference relevant previous papers here.  
  *   
* If you’re training, what dataset will you use for that?  
* What is the evaluation metric(s)?   
  * Some tasks are straightforward to measure (e.g accuracy, for mathematical reasoning). Some are much harder (e.g LLM persuasive ability \- think about how you would do this).

**Datasets:** 

* Self-supervised Training: TUH EEG Corpus: Largest Medical Dataset with EEG data, Sleep-EDF: Contains EEG data from sleeping patients  
* Supervised Datasets: ds004148 \- EEG test-retest, ds002778 \- UC San Diego, ds003490 \- EEG 3-Stim, ds004584 \- EEG PD  
  * Datasets referenced from TransformEEG (Del Pup et al., 2025\)

**Metrics:** Aiming for Balanced Accuracy/Precision in patient cases, lower IQR around 6%, Specificity and Sensitivity in real Parkinson’s cases.

# Benchmarks/Evaluation Sets

* Evaluation and comparison with existing systems.  
  * What are your baselines? 

**Benchmark/Evaluation:** We will Evaluate the Self-Supervised model of the TransformEEG model against the original datasets used:  ds004148 \- EEG test-retest, ds002778 \- UC San Diego, ds003490 \- EEG 3-Stim, ds004584 \- EEG PD. The baseline models tested against the State-of-the-art supervised TransformEEG model as well as a SSL-EEG pretrained model (need fine-tuned) to detect Parkinson’s disease. 

# Ideal Results

* What’s the best case scenario/what do you hope to demonstrate?  
  * What is your hypothesis? What results would prove it to be true?

We believe that through the use of self-supervised learning, TransformEEG will be able to improve its generalizability and robustness in Parkinson’s disease detection. We want to use larger unlabeled datasets along with the original labeled datasets from the TransformEEG paper to see how much it can improve due to self-supervised learning. Ideally, we would see a noticeable increase in performance so that it can be applied and utilized in real world clinical scenarios.

# Potential Limitations

1. There is a possibility of overfitting during the fine tuning process due to the datasets being too small or imbalanced since there is a lack of data in this field.  
2. EEG architectures and augmentations require much more computational complexity as it must be designed carefully and does not work the same as other vision based SSL methods.  
3. While the TransformEEG architecture may improve from self-supervised settings, the original architecture may not be fully optimized for SSL, which may prove to make our work more challenging.
